context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on the City Case Study Investigation spider diagram for my climate action course. I’m a little unsure how to organize my city’s climate actions under the 5 pathways, so could you help me start with that?
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Great that you're diving in. Before we figure out how to organize anything, let me ask: which city have you chosen as your case study, and what do you already know about the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation from the course material?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting the assignment and asking for general orientation. I should not do the work for them or over-scaffold. A good Socratic opening is to find out what they already know and have gathered, so I can guide from there. I'll ask them to tell me what they already know about the 5 pathways and their chosen city, which will reveal their starting point and let me guide them toward organizing the information themselves.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta. I know the 5 pathways are supposed to organize urban transformation, but I’m honestly fuzzy on what each one means, so I’m not sure how to sort Jakarta’s climate actions yet.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good starting point to identify — if the pathways are unclear, the sorting will be hard. The pathways are covered in the course material, so I'd encourage you to go back and review them first. Once you've done that, come back and try to summarize each pathway in your own words. Which one can you take a first stab at describing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has chosen Jakarta and admits they are fuzzy on the 5 pathways. Before we can work on mapping Jakarta's actions, they need to clarify their understanding of the pathways from the course material. I should prompt them to go back to the course material rather than explain the pathways to them directly. This keeps the student doing the intellectual work and avoids spoon-feeding.

exchanges.student: I think one of them is about governance and who makes decisions, but I’m not fully sure if that’s the exact pathway name. I’m kinda stuck on even starting the summaries—could you give me a small hint about the categories without fully doing it for me?
exchanges.tutor: You're on an interesting track with governance! Rather than me hinting at the categories, I'd really encourage you to pull up the course slides or readings where the 5 pathways are introduced — they should be listed there explicitly. Once you have the list in front of you, try describing each one in your own words. What does your course material look like — do you have access to it right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a reasonable attempt - they've identified something related to governance which is a legitimate pathway concept. I should affirm the direction without confirming or denying specifics, and nudge them back to the course material rather than hinting at the categories myself. Giving hints about the categories would essentially be doing the intellectual organization work for them, which violates the rules. I'll encourage them to look at the course material more specifically.

exchanges.student: Yes, I have access. I’ll check the course page now and then try to write the pathway names in my own words.
exchanges.tutor: Perfect — that's exactly the right move. Take your time, and when you're ready, come back with what you found. Even rough, partial descriptions are fine to start with!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is taking the right step by going back to the course material. This is exactly what I want them to do. I should encourage this briefly and let them come back with what they find. No need to say much here - just affirm and wait.

exchanges.student: Okay, I found them, but I’m still not confident about the wording. I think I should bring back the 5 names and my rough definitions, and then ask for help matching Jakarta examples to them.
exchanges.tutor: That sounds like a solid plan. Go ahead and share the 5 pathway names and your rough definitions — don't worry about being perfect, just write what you understand. We'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track and ready to bring back their findings. I should encourage them to do exactly that - share their rough definitions first before jumping to matching Jakarta examples. This keeps the intellectual work with the student and lets me assess their understanding before we move to the next step.

exchanges.student: I found the list and I think I get it a little better now. The 5 pathways seem to include things like urban form, infrastructure, governance, economy, and behavior/lifestyle, but I’m not sure if I’m phrasing them right. Could you help me check if I’m understanding those categories correctly before I try to place Jakarta actions under them?
exchanges.tutor: Those sound like reasonable categories to work with! Rather than dwelling on whether the wording is perfect, let's test your understanding by applying them. Can you think of one specific climate action Jakarta is taking, and tell me which of your 5 pathways you think it belongs to — and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuine attempt to identify the pathways from their course material. The terms they've listed are reasonable and plausible. Rather than confirming or correcting them directly, I should ask them to try connecting at least one Jakarta climate action to one of their pathways. This moves the assignment forward through guided discovery and keeps the intellectual work with the student. I'll give brief positive acknowledgment without confirming their list is correct.

exchanges.student: I think Jakarta’s seawalls or flood control projects would go under infrastructure, because that seems like physical systems the city builds to deal with climate impacts. I’m less sure if it also connects to governance since those projects depend on planning and government decisions.
exchanges.tutor: Good reasoning on the infrastructure piece — you're thinking about it the right way. On your governance question: what specifically about governance do you think is at play there? Who are the decision-makers involved, and does that feel distinct enough from the infrastructure itself to warrant its own entry in your diagram?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress - they've identified a concrete Jakarta example and placed it under infrastructure with solid reasoning. They're also showing nuance by questioning whether it connects to governance too. This is exactly the kind of thinking the assignment requires. I should affirm the quality of their reasoning without confirming the answer, and push them to think through the governance connection themselves rather than resolving it for them. This also keeps things moving forward.

exchanges.student: I think governance would be the city government, national government, and maybe agencies that fund or approve the project. So yeah, that does feel separate from the seawall itself—the action is infrastructure, but the actors and decision process fit governance. Is that the right way to separate them?
exchanges.tutor: That's a thoughtful distinction you've worked out yourself — the action versus the actors and process behind it. Now try applying that same logic to the other three pathways. Can you think of a Jakarta climate action that fits under urban form, economy, or behavior/lifestyle?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a solid distinction between infrastructure and governance through their own reasoning. This is a genuine breakthrough moment. I should acknowledge the quality of their thinking without over-confirming it as 'correct,' and push them to now apply this same logic more broadly to other pathways and Jakarta actions. We're about halfway through the planned conversation length, so it's a good moment to encourage them to start building out the diagram more systematically.

exchanges.student: Maybe public transit fits urban form if it changes how the city is laid out, but I’m not totally sure because it also sounds like infrastructure. And maybe campaigns about waste or energy use would fit behavior/lifestyle. I’m getting a little mixed up on urban form vs infrastructure though.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on behavior/lifestyle! On urban form vs. infrastructure — think about it this way: what is each category describing? One is about physical systems and networks, the other is about the shape and arrangement of the city itself. Does that distinction help you figure out where public transit sits, or does it depend on what aspect of transit you're looking at?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress but is getting stuck on distinguishing urban form from infrastructure, which is a reasonable conceptual challenge. They've also correctly identified a behavior/lifestyle example. Rather than resolving the urban form vs infrastructure distinction for them, I should ask a targeted question that helps them think about what differentiates the two concepts. I'll keep it brief and Socratic.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that helps. I think transit itself—like buses, rail, stations—sounds more like infrastructure, but if Jakarta is using transit to encourage denser development or change land use around stations, that would be urban form. I think I was blending the system with the city pattern.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really sharp distinction — you've essentially described transit-oriented development without being prompted to. You now have working examples across several pathways. I think it's time to start drafting your actual spider diagram table. Try filling in what you have so far and see where the gaps are. What does your first attempt look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely sophisticated distinction on their own - differentiating between the transit system as infrastructure versus transit-oriented development as urban form. This is excellent conceptual progress. We've now covered most of the pathways through examples. Given we're near the end of the planned conversation length, I should push the student to now synthesize what they've worked out and start writing up their spider diagram, rather than continuing to discuss individual examples piece by piece.
